Title: Draft genome sequence of Camellia sinensis var. sinensis provides insights into the evolution of the tea genome and tea quality
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Pathway_Genes.Gene_Name: SCPL1A (type 1A serine carboxypeptidase-like acyltransferases; tea-specific expanded members)
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Galloylated catechins, especially ECG and EGCG
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a galloyl group in a 1-O-glucose ester-dependent reaction to flavan-3-ols to form galloylated catechins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major determinants of tea quality, taste/astringency, and health-promoting properties
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Most members highly expressed in apical buds and young leaves; some other members expressed in roots and flowers
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Most members highly expressed in apical buds and young leaves
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with high accumulation of galloylated catechins, particularly EGCG and ECG, in young tea tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative genomics; phylogenetic analysis; transcriptome-metabolite correlation analysis across tissues
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing; RNA-seq; metabolite profiling by HPLC and LC-Q-TOF-MS; PacBio transcript data; accession NA (website-hosted dataset)
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate targets for breeding or metabolic engineering to enhance galloylated catechin content and tea quality, but direct functional validation of individual members is still needed
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: 22 SCPL genes were identified in tea; 15 expanded through recent tea-specific tandem duplication and their expression strongly correlated with EGCG/ECG accumulation. Authors note functional experimentation is still needed to prove which individual SCPL genes catalyze galloylation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The SCPL1A acyltransferase family is markedly expanded in tea, mainly through recent tandem duplication. 2. Most tea-specific SCPL1A genes are highly expressed in apical buds and young leaves where galloylated catechins accumulate. 3. Expression of 14 tea-specific SCPL genes is significantly correlated with EGCG and ECG accumulation, implicating them in catechin galloylation.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Trans-flavan-3-ols (catechin-type products); associated with monomeric catechin pool
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies target-product pool and influences monomer/polymer balance
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to tea quality and palatability
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in buds and young leaves; lower in roots and flowers
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in buds and young leaves; lower in roots and flowers
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: High LAR expression is associated with monomeric catechin accumulation and reduced proanthocyanidin polymerization in young tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic/evolutionary analysis; tissue transcript profiling; metabolite correlation analysis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Lower expression in roots/flowers is associated with greater polymerized PA accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing; RNA-seq; metabolite profiling by HPLC and LC-Q-TOF-MS; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to favor monomeric catechin composition and tea palatability
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors discuss consistency with Medicago evidence that LAR can reduce PA oligomerization by converting extension units back to starter units; in tea this is inferred from expression-metabolite relationships rather than direct enzyme tests in this paper
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Tea LAR family members expanded during genome evolution. 2. LAR expression is high in buds and young leaves where monomeric catechins are abundant. 3. Lower LAR expression in roots and flowers coincides with higher polymerized proanthocyanidins, suggesting a role in monomer/polymer balance.

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor/product pool contributing to galloylated catechins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to tea quality and palatability
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Specific members correlated with cis-flavan-3-ol accumulation across tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Specific members correlated with cis-flavan-3-ol accumulation across tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with tissue accumulation of cis-flavan-3-ols, key precursors/components of tea catechins
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic/evolutionary analysis; tissue transcript profiling; metabolite correlation analysis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing; RNA-seq; metabolite profiling; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate target for tuning cis-catechin composition in tea
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Inference is based on correlation with tissue catechin profiles, not direct functional assays in this study
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ANR genes were identified as key members of the tea catechin biosynthetic network. 2. Expression of particular ANR family members correlates with cis-flavan-3-ol accumulation in tissues. 3. ANR likely contributes to tea-specific catechin composition together with LAR and SCPL genes.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Core flavonoid pathway feeding flavan-3-ol branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor for downstream catechin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to tea quality and palatability
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Specific members show tissue expression correlated with catechin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Specific members show tissue expression correlated with catechin accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with catechin biosynthesis capacity in tea tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene family evolution analysis; transcriptome-metabolite correlation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing; RNA-seq; metabolite profiling; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential upstream engineering target for catechin enhancement, likely best used with downstream branch genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: One pair of DFR genes appears duplicated within last 2–25 My
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DFR family members expanded in tea and were impacted by WGD/paralogous duplication. 2. Expression of specific DFR members correlates with catechin accumulation. 3. DFR supplies leucoanthocyanidin precursors for downstream LAR/ANR-mediated catechin production.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Type III polyketide synthase chalcone synthase
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Upstream entry step of flavonoid branch
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for catechin biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to tea quality and palatability
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Specific members correlate with catechin-related accumulation and TF coexpression
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Specific members correlate with catechin-related accumulation and TF coexpression
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with catechin biosynthetic flux in tea tissues
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene family evolution analysis; transcriptome-metabolite correlation; coexpression network analysis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing; RNA-seq; metabolite profiling; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful upstream target for increasing flavonoid flux, though broad pathway effects are likely
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Two CHS gene pairs were inferred to duplicate within last 2–25 My
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CHS genes were identified as part of expanded catechin-related structural gene families in tea. 2. Specific CHS members show expression patterns linked to catechin-producing tissues. 3. CHS genes are embedded in coexpression networks with multiple TF families regulating catechin biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Camellia sinensis var. sinensis; Arabidopsis thaliana
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: GSI-type glutamine synthetase-like protein with C-terminal GLN-SYN motif and N-terminal amidohydrolase domain
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses glutamate and ethylamine to synthesize theanine
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Major tea quality and health-related compound; important flavor component and nitrogen reservoir
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots primarily; also young buds and tender leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in roots; induced by ethylamine feeding, peaking at 12 d
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Tea roots high; overexpressed transgenically in Arabidopsis seedlings under 35S promoter
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes theanine biosynthesis; overexpression enables strong theanine production in Arabidopsis only when ethylamine is supplied
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic analysis; tissue expression-metabolite correlation; ethylamine feeding response; heterologous overexpression in Arabidopsis with metabolite assay
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ethylamine feeding in hydroponic tea seedlings and Arabidopsis seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Genome sequencing; RNA-seq; amino acid profiling; accession NA
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for engineering or breeding elevated theanine content; likely requires adequate ethylamine supply and coupling with nitrogen metabolism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Authors renamed the candidate GSI-like gene CsGSI to CsTSI based on homology to Pseudomonas taetrolens GS used for theanine production; paper states this result proves CsTSI has theanine synthetase activity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CsTSI was identified as the best candidate theanine synthetase gene based on phylogeny and homology. 2. CsTSI is highly expressed in roots, matching root-biased theanine synthesis and accumulation patterns, and is induced by ethylamine feeding. 3. Overexpression of CsTSI in Arabidopsis causes strong theanine production upon ethylamine supply, demonstrating theanine synthetase activity.

Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CHS; DFR; ANS; ANR; SCPL and other catechin biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Expression patterns closely related to structural catechin gene expression, direction not individually resolved
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Contributors to complex TF network associated with catechin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Associated with bud/leaf expression of catechin genes
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Associated with bud/leaf expression of catechin genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with regulation of catechin biosynthetic gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Coexpression network analysis using transcriptome and metabolome datasets
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory targets for coordinated manipulation of catechin biosynthesis, though individual causal TFs remain unresolved
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: TF identities are presented as family-level associations rather than individually functionally validated genes in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MADS-box TF family members are coexpressed with catechin structural genes. 2. Their expression relationships suggest involvement in catechin biosynthetic regulation in buds and leaves. 3. Evidence is correlative rather than direct functional validation.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation/repression inferred from known flavonoid regulatory roles and coexpression
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CHS; DFR; ANS; ANR; SCPL; PAL
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Associated with expression of catechin biosynthesis genes; specific activating or repressing members not resolved here
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important components of flavonoid regulatory network controlling catechin synthesis
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Known MYB-bHLH-WD40 ternary complex concept discussed, but not directly shown for individual tea genes here
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: bHLH TFs; WD40 proteins (general flavonoid regulatory framework)
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Closely related to expression of catechin biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Closely related to expression of catechin biosynthetic genes
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with regulation of catechin accumulation through structural gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Coexpression network analysis; literature-supported functional inference
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Promising regulators for coordinated enhancement or rebalancing of catechin composition, but this paper does not identify single validated master MYB genes
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Paper emphasizes a more complex regulatory system than the canonical MYB-bHLH-WD40 model alone
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. R2R3-MYB TFs are strongly coexpressed with catechin structural genes. 2. They likely participate in the regulatory network modulating catechin biosynthesis in tea leaves. 3. Specific causal MYB genes were not functionally dissected in this study.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional regulation inferred by coexpression and known flavonoid complex roles
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: CHS; DFR; ANS; ANR; SCPL and other catechin pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Associated with catechin structural gene expression patterns
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Part of putative flavonoid regulatory machinery in tea
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely part of MYB-bHLH-WD40 complexes based on known pathway logic, but not individually validated in this paper
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Coexpressed with catechin biosynthetic genes in buds/leaves
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Coexpressed with catechin biosynthetic genes in buds/leaves
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with regulation of catechin biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulators for coordinated pathway control, pending validation of specific members
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bHLH TFs show expression relationships with catechin biosynthetic genes. 2. They likely contribute to catechin regulation in conjunction with other TF families. 3. No single bHLH gene was directly functionally validated here.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: WRKY; C2H2 zinc finger; C3H zinc finger; NAC; ERF
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional regulation inferred by strong coexpression association
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Catechin biosynthesis linked to stress adaptation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Catechin biosynthesis genes including SCPL, PAL, DFR, CHS and related modules
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Strong association with expression of catechin pathway genes; direction not resolved
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-associated regulators linking stress responses with catechin production
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense against insects and microbes; adaptation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Strong association with catechin gene expression in tea tissues
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Strong association with catechin gene expression in tea tissues
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Suggests catechin pathway is integrated with stress-responsive transcriptional control
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Coexpression network analysis; comparative interpretation with prior biological knowledge
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May link metabolite accumulation with stress-response programs
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential route to couple catechin quality traits with stress resilience, but direct causal genes remain unvalidated
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These are family-level associations only; no single TF gene was established as core causal regulator by direct experiment
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Stress-related TF families WRKY, C2H2, C3H, NAC, and ERF are strongly associated with catechin biosynthetic gene expression. 2. This supports a link between catechin production and stress adaptation in tea. 3. Evidence is correlative and network-based.
